slides.title: Personal AI Agents
slides.subtitle: Building AI That Knows You
slides.notes: Welcome everyone. I'm Michael Kilman — software engineer, and I build with AI every day. Today I want to show you something I've been working on that I think represents the future of how we'll all interact with AI.

quote.text: Every conversation starts from zero. You're always re-explaining who you are, what you're working on, what you care about.

slides.notes: Raise your hand if you've used ChatGPT. Now keep it up if you've ever had to re-explain the same context for the third time. That frustration — that's the cold start problem. Every session is amnesia. Today I want to show you what happens when you solve it.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: AI Without Memory Is Just a Calculator
slides.subtitle: It can answer questions, but it doesn't know you
slides.notes: Think about the difference between asking a stranger for directions versus asking a friend. The friend knows where you live, where you work, what route you usually take. That context changes everything.

slides.title: What Is a Personal Agent?

sub: Knows you, reaches you anywhere, acts on your behalf

slides.notes: This is the key distinction. A chatbot is a tool. An assistant is someone else's tool that you're allowed to use. A personal agent is YOUR tool — you own it, you control it, it grows with you.

slides.label: Professional Context
slides.title: AI at Scale — beehiiv
slides.subtitle: What building production AI systems taught me
slides.notes: Quick context on my day job. I'm a software engineer at beehiiv — one of the largest newsletter platforms. We use AI in production every day, serving millions of users. That experience building reliable AI systems is what led me to ask: what would it look like to build something this robust, but personal?

slides.title: Lessons from Production AI
bullets: AI is most powerful when it has context about the user
Reliability matters more than cleverness
The best AI systems are invisible — they just work
Ownership of your AI relationship matters
slides.notes: These four lessons are what drove everything I built. Context, reliability, invisibility, ownership. Keep these in mind as I show you the three pillars.

slides.label: The Three Pillars
slides.title: What Makes a Personal Agent
slides.subtitle: Accessibility · Memory · Autonomy
slides.notes: Every personal agent needs three things. Let me tell you a story about each one.

slides.label: Pillar 1
slides.title: Accessibility
slides.subtitle: Reach your agent from anywhere, on any device
slides.notes: I'm on my couch and I text Claudia on iMessage to remind me about something. Next morning I'm at my desk and pick up the same thread in the web UI. On a walk, I talk to her through voice on my phone. Same agent, everywhere.

slides.label: Pillar 1 — Accessibility
slides.title: One Brain, Many Interfaces
bullets: Web UI — browser-based chat
CLI — terminal for developers
VS Code — sidebar in your editor
macOS Menubar — quick access app
iOS App — native voice mode
iMessage — text-based, always on
Voice — real-time streaming TTS
slides.notes: Seven interfaces, one agent. The key insight: the agent shouldn't care HOW you talk to it. A single gateway serves everything — WebSocket, web UI, extensions — on one port.

slides.label: Pillar 2
slides.title: Memory
slides.subtitle: An agent that remembers every conversation automatically
slides.notes: This is the one that blows people away. I never told Claudia to remember my preferences, my projects, my patterns. She has a librarian — we call her Libby — who reads every conversation and distills what matters.

slides.label: Pillar 2 — Memory
slides.title: The Memory Pipeline

sub: Reads every conversation, distills what matters
Not just summaries — full stories of what happened
Every new session starts with relevant memories

slides.notes: The agent learns about you passively. You don't 'train' it. Libby processes conversations in the background, extracts milestones, insights, timeline events. When you start a new conversation, relevant context is automatically injected.

slides.label: Memory System — By the Numbers
slides.title: A Living Archive
slides.notes: 4,000+ archived conversation summaries and 500+ memory documents — all indexed with SQLite FTS5 in under one second on startup. This is real, running right now on my machine.

slides.label: Pillar 3
slides.title: Autonomy
slides.subtitle: An agent that acts without waiting for a prompt
slides.notes: The third pillar is what separates a personal agent from a chatbot. I don't always have to ask. Claudia runs scheduled tasks, reacts to webhooks, processes things in the background.

slides.label: Pillar 3 — Autonomy
slides.title: Beyond Prompt-Response

sub: Memory ingestion runs autonomously — no prompting needed
Respond to external events in real-time
Alert me when something important happens

slides.notes: The agent isn't passive. It's always running, always watching, always ready. The memory pipeline is a perfect example — it processes conversations without me lifting a finger.

slides.title: Live Demo
slides.subtitle: Let's see it in action
slides.notes: Alright, enough slides. Let me show you the real thing.

slides.title: Demo Time
slides.subtitle: Watch the pillars in action
bullets: Claude Code — the AI coding agent foundation
Web UI — real conversation with streaming
Memory recall — asking about past conversations
iMessage — texting Claudia live on stage
Developer workflow — git hooks, automated checks
slides.notes: Start with Claude Code in terminal (30 sec). Show web UI conversation. Ask a memory question: 'When did I start working on the memory system?' Show iMessage from phone. If time, show git hooks / dev workflow.

quote.text: By the way — this slide deck you're looking at? It's not PowerPoint. It's not Google Slides. It's a Claudia extension. She generated the slides, and this extension is rendering them right now.

slides.notes: THIS is the mic drop. Pause here. Let it sink in. The presentation about Claudia is being presented BY Claudia. Everything they're seeing — the rendering, the slide transitions, the speaker notes — is running as a Claudia extension on port 30086.

slides.title: The Bigger Picture
slides.subtitle: Where personal agents are heading
slides.notes: Let me zoom out for a moment and talk about why this matters beyond my personal project.

slides.title: The Landscape Is Shifting
bullets: Claude Code, Codex, Gemini — agents are becoming the interface
OpenClaw — the open-source personal agent movement
Memory and persistence are becoming table stakes
The question isn't if, it's who owns it
slides.notes: Major AI labs are all moving toward agent-based interfaces. This isn't a hobby project anymore — it's the direction the entire industry is heading. The question is whether you'll build your own, or use someone else's.

quote.text: The question isn't whether AI will be personal. It's whether you'll own that personal AI, or whether a company will own it for you.

slides.notes: End on this. For educators especially — think about what this means for your students. The students who learn to build and own their AI tools will have a fundamentally different relationship with technology than those who just consume it.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: Questions?
slides.notes: Open the floor. Be ready for: How much does it cost? Is it private? Can I build one? How does Claude Code work? What about hallucinations?
